title: Nephrolithiasis: Diagnosis and Management
objectives: Recognize the classic presentation of renal colic
Understand stone composition and risk factors
Apply appropriate imaging and laboratory workup

sections.title: Epidemiology & Risk Factors
sections.content:
**Prevalence:** 10-15% lifetime risk

**Risk Factors:**
• Low fluid intake (most important modifiable factor)
• High sodium diet
• High animal protein intake
• Obesity and metabolic syndrome
• Family history (2.5x risk)
• Hot climates (dehydration)
• Certain medications (topiramate, indinavir)

**Recurrence:** 50% within 5-10 years without intervention

sections.title: Stone Composition
sections.content:
**Calcium Oxalate (75-80%):**
• Most common type
• Envelope-shaped crystals on UA
• Associated with hypercalciuria, hyperoxaluria

**Calcium Phosphite (15%):**
• Associated with RTA type 1
• Alkaline urine

**Uric Acid (5-10%):**
• Radiolucent on X-ray
• Acidic urine (pH <5.5)
• Gout, myeloproliferative disorders
• Can be dissolved with alkalinization

**Struvite (5-10%):**
• 'Coffin lid' crystals
• UTI with urease-producing bacteria (Proteus)
• Can form staghorn calculi

**Cystine (1-2%):**
• Hexagonal crystals
• Autosomal recessive cystinuria
• Positive cyanide-nitroprusside test

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
**Renal Colic:**
• Sudden onset, severe, colicky flank pain
• Radiates to groin/testicle/labia
• Patient unable to find comfortable position
• Nausea and vomiting
• Hematuria (80-90%)

**Pain Location by Stone Position:**
• Upper ureter/UPJ: Flank pain
• Mid-ureter: Lower quadrant pain
• Distal ureter/UVJ: Suprapubic, dysuria, urgency

**Red Flags:**
• Fever (infected stone - emergency!)
• Solitary kidney
• Bilateral obstruction
• Acute kidney injury

sections.title: Diagnostic Workup
sections.content:
**Imaging:**
• Non-contrast CT (gold standard) - 95% sensitivity
• KUB X-ray: Limited, misses radiolucent stones
• Ultrasound: First-line in pregnancy, children

**Laboratory:**
• Urinalysis: Hematuria, pH, crystals
• BMP: Creatinine, electrolytes
• CBC: Infection screening
• Urine culture if febrile

**24-Hour Urine Collection:**
• For recurrent stone formers
• Identifies metabolic abnormalities
• Guides preventive therapy

sections.title: Management - Conservative
sections.content:
**Medical Expulsive Therapy (MET):**
• Alpha-blockers (tamsulosin 0.4mg daily)
• Increases spontaneous passage rate
• Most effective for distal ureteral stones

**Spontaneous Passage Rates by Size:**
• <5mm: 68-98% pass spontaneously
• 5-10mm: 47% pass
• >10mm: Unlikely to pass

**Supportive Care:**
• Aggressive hydration
• NSAIDs (ketorolac) - first-line analgesia
• Antiemetics
• Strain urine for stone analysis

sections.title: Management - Surgical
sections.content:
**Indications for Intervention:**
• Stone >10mm
• Failure to progress after 4-6 weeks
• Uncontrolled pain
• Infection with obstruction (EMERGENCY)
• Acute kidney injury
• Solitary kidney

**Options:**
• **SWL (Extracorporeal Shock Wave Lithotripsy):** Non-invasive, stones <2cm
• **Ureteroscopy:** Direct visualization, laser lithotripsy
• **PCNL:** Large/staghorn calculi

**Emergency Decompression:**
• Infected obstructing stone = sepsis risk
• Ureteral stent or nephrostomy tube
• Antibiotics before definitive treatment

sections.title: Prevention
sections.content:
**Universal Recommendations:**
• Increase fluid intake (>2.5L urine output/day)
• Reduce sodium intake
• Moderate animal protein
• Maintain normal calcium intake (not low!)

**Stone-Specific Prevention:**
• Calcium oxalate: Thiazide diuretics, potassium citrate
• Uric acid: Alkalinize urine (K-citrate), allopurinol
• Cystine: High fluid, alkalinization, tiopronin
• Struvite: Treat/prevent UTIs, complete stone removal

keyTakeaways: Non-contrast CT is the gold standard for diagnosis
Infected obstructing stone is a urologic emergency
24-hour urine collection guides prevention in recurrent formers
Increased fluid intake is the most important preventive measure

references.keyContent: Comprehensive overview of kidney stone diagnosis and management

references.citation: AUA Guideline: Surgical Management of Stones
AUA Guideline: Medical Management of Kidney Stones

reviewQuestions.question: A 35-year-old man presents with acute left flank pain, nausea, and hematuria. CT shows a 4mm distal ureteral stone. He is afebrile with normal renal function. What is the most appropriate initial management?
reviewQuestions.explanation: A 4mm distal ureteral stone has a high likelihood of spontaneous passage (>90%). Medical expulsive therapy with tamsulosin, adequate hydration, and pain control is appropriate first-line management. Surgery is reserved for failure to progress or complications.

reviewQuestions.question: A patient with a known ureteral stone develops fever to 39°C and rigors. Labs show leukocytosis and elevated creatinine. What is the most important next step?
options: Start IV antibiotics and schedule outpatient ureteroscopy
Emergency ureteral stent or nephrostomy tube placement
reviewQuestions.explanation: Infected obstructing stone is a urologic emergency with high risk of sepsis and death. Emergency decompression with ureteral stent or nephrostomy tube is required, along with IV antibiotics. Definitive stone treatment occurs after infection resolves.

reviewQuestions.question: Which stone type is radiolucent on plain radiograph and can potentially be dissolved with medical therapy?
reviewQuestions.explanation: Uric acid stones are radiolucent on X-ray (visible only on CT) and form in acidic urine. They can be dissolved by alkalinizing the urine to pH 6.5-7.0 with potassium citrate. This is unique among common stone types.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected kidney stones?

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in kidney stones. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.
Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.
Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Obtain a thorough history and physical examination.
correct.deeperWhy: Understanding this concept is foundational for clinical reasoning in this domain.
correct.clinicalPearl: Remember: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination

coachDx.misconception: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.
coachDx.guidance: Review the key features that distinguish Obtain a thorough history and physical examination from Consult a specialist before any workup.
coachDx.bridgeTo: The key distinction involves Obtain a thorough history and physical examination.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of kidney stones. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.
Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.
While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Consider the differential diagnosis systematically.
correct.deeperWhy: Understanding this concept is foundational for clinical reasoning in this domain.
correct.clinicalPearl: Remember: Consider the differential diagnosis systematically

coachDx.misconception: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.
coachDx.guidance: Review the key features that distinguish Consider the differential diagnosis systematically from Order every available test to rule out all possibilities.
coachDx.bridgeTo: The key distinction involves Consider the differential diagnosis systematically.

mcqs.stem: When managing kidney stones, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values.
correct.deeperWhy: Understanding this concept is foundational for clinical reasoning in this domain.
correct.clinicalPearl: Remember: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values

coachDx.misconception: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.
coachDx.guidance: Review the key features that distinguish Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values from Always defer to the most recent published study.
coachDx.bridgeTo: The key distinction involves Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values.

mcqs.stem: A patient has the following ABG: pH 7.28, pCO2 24 mmHg, HCO3 11 mEq/L. Serum Na 140, Cl 100, K 4.0, albumin 4.0. What is the acid-base interpretation?

options.text: Mixed anion gap metabolic acidosis and respiratory alkalosis
options.explanation: The pCO2 of 24 falls within Winter's formula range (22.5-26.5), indicating appropriate compensation rather than a primary respiratory alkalosis.

options.text: Non-anion gap metabolic acidosis (NAGMA) with appropriate respiratory compensation
options.explanation: Anion gap = Na - (Cl + HCO3) = 140 - (100 + 11) = 29. This is elevated (normal 12 ± 4), indicating an anion gap metabolic acidosis, not NAGMA.

options.text: Anion gap metabolic acidosis with concurrent non-anion gap metabolic acidosis
options.explanation: Delta-delta ratio is ~1.3 (17/13), which falls in the 1-2 range indicating pure AGMA. A ratio <1 would suggest concurrent NAGMA.

options.text: Anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
options.explanation: AG = 140 - (100+11) = 29 (elevated). Expected pCO2 by Winter's formula = 1.5(11) + 8 ± 2 = 22.5-26.5. Actual pCO2 of 24 is within this range, confirming appropriate respiratory compensation. Calculate delta-delta: Δ AG = 29-12 = 17; Δ HCO3 = 24-11 = 13. Ratio ~1.3 suggests pure AGMA without concurrent non-AG acidosis or metabolic alkalosis.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation.
correct.deeperWhy: Understanding this concept is foundational for clinical reasoning in this domain.
correct.clinicalPearl: Remember: Anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

coachDx.misconception: Delta-delta ratio is ~1.3 (17/13), which falls in the 1-2 range indicating pure AGMA. A ratio <1 would suggest concurrent NAGMA.
coachDx.guidance: Review the key features that distinguish Anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation from Anion gap metabolic acidosis with concurrent non-anion gap metabolic acidosis.
coachDx.bridgeTo: The key distinction involves Anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation.
